evidence_quotes.quote: destruction of video games by publishers, i.e. situations in which legally purchased games cease to operate after the end of support or the shutdown of servers.
Today, more and more games require a fixed online connection or activation by the manufacturer’s servers.

paragraphs.text: Ladies and gentlemen, I would like to draw your attention to an important gap in the area of digital justice and consumer protection, which has not yet been addressed in the draft Digital Justice Act. This is the problem of the destruction of video games by publishers, i.e. situations in which legally purchased games cease to operate after the end of support or the shutdown of servers. Today, more and more games require a fixed online connection or activation by the manufacturer’s servers. When the publisher decides to exclude them, the consumer is deprived of access to the product for which he has paid. This means that the right to use the game purchased is illusory and depends on the unilateral decision of the manufacturer. Such practices run counter to the fundamental principles of fair trade and consumer protection. The issue is not the transfer of copyright or source code, but the provision of minimum guarantees of use: e.g. offline mode, an update removing the requirement of a server, or any other solution that allows the player to use the game even after the support has been completed. More than 1440000 EU citizens have already seen this problem and supported the European Citizens’ Initiative Stop Destroying Videogames. This shows that this is a real and widespread problem that deserves to be taken into account in the legislative work. That is why I call for the Digital Fairness Act to consider introducing provisions to protect consumers from losing access to the video games they have bought legally. Only then will consumer protection in the digital world really be fully protected.
